Predictive Modeling of Movie Gross Revenue

Student Name

2025-08-20

  • Library and Data Initialization
  • a) Preprocess the data
    • Data Structure and Summary
    • Missing Data Analysis
    • Investigating the Missing Data Mechanism
    • Data Imputation
    • Post-Imputation Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
  • b) Split the dataset
  • c) Model selection
    • Initial Full Model
    • Feature Selection
    • Diagnostics of the Selected Model
    • Outlier Detection and Removal
    • Test for Outlier Removal
    • Re-selection After Outlier Removal
    • Variable Transformations
  • Predictor Transformations: Power Transform for Non-Categorical Variables
  • Box-Cox for Gross
  • Re-Check Assumptions for Final Transformed Model
    • Testing for Interaction Effects
    • Final Best Model and Diagnostics
  • d) Prediction
  • e) Comparison and conclusion
    • Validation Set Performance
    • Repeated Cross-Validation Performance
  • f) Improvements or alternative methods
    • Alternative Method: XGBoost

This report details the process of building a predictive model for movie gross revenue using the Conventional and Social Media Movies (CSM) dataset. The analysis covers comprehensive data preprocessing, robust model selection and validation, assumption checking, and a comparison with an alternative machine learning approach.

Library and Data Initialization

First, we load all necessary R libraries and the dataset. For this document to be self-contained and reproducible, the dataset file (CSM.xlsx) should be located in the same directory as this R Markdown file.

# Import libraries for the entire analysis
library(ggplot2)      
library(corrplot)     
library(car)          
library(naniar)       
library(VIM)          
library(MASS)         
library(lmtest)       
library(dplyr)        
library(leaps)        
library(skimr)        
library(GGally)       
library(missForest)   
library(caret)        
library(xgboost)      
library(effects)
library(openxlsx)

# Load the dataset from the CSV file.
# The original script used read.xlsx; we use read.csv for the provided file format.
data_raw <- read.xlsx("CSM.xlsx")

# Remove the final empty row that exists in the CSV file
data_raw <- data_raw[rowSums(is.na(data_raw)) != ncol(data_raw), ]

# Display the dimensions and a preview of the data
cat("Dataset dimensions:", dim(data_raw), "\n")
## Dataset dimensions: 231 14
head(data_raw)
##                                   Movie Year Ratings Genre    Gross   Budget
## 1                        Jurassic World 2015     7.3     1 6.43e+08 1.50e+08
## 2               Avengers: Age of Ultron 2015     7.8     1 4.58e+08 2.50e+08
## 3                       American Sniper 2014     7.3     1 3.50e+08 5.88e+07
## 4                             Furious 7 2015     7.4     1 3.50e+08 1.90e+08
## 5                            Inside Out 2015     8.6    12 3.45e+08 1.75e+08
## 6 The Hunger Games: Mockingjay - Part 1 2014     6.8     2 3.37e+08 1.25e+08
##   Screens Sequel Sentiment    Views Likes Dislikes Comments Aggregate.Followers
## 1    4274      4         1  9143740 34746     1074     5107             6180000
## 2    4276      2        21 10366624 31552      989     3843            10070000
## 3    3555      1         4  3927600 13143      573     3134               24300
## 4    4004      7       -17    59056   330        8       39            11890000
## 5    3946      1         2  1438926  4632      262      496              232000
## 6    4151      3         2  3305417 14684      332     1176            31030000

a) Preprocess the data

Preprocessing is a critical first step to ensure data quality and prepare the dataset for modeling. This involves understanding the data structure, handling missing values, and performing initial exploratory data analysis.

Data Structure and Summary

We start by examining the structure and statistical summaries of each variable.

# str() provides a compact view of the data frame's structure and variable types.
str(data_raw)
## 'data.frame':    231 obs. of  14 variables:
##  $ Movie              : chr  "Jurassic World" "Avengers: Age of Ultron" "American Sniper" "Furious 7" ...
##  $ Year               : num  2015 2015 2014 2015 2015 ...
##  $ Ratings            : num  7.3 7.8 7.3 7.4 8.6 6.8 8.1 6.6 7.8 7.8 ...
##  $ Genre              : num  1 1 1 1 12 2 1 1 1 12 ...
##  $ Gross              : num  6.43e+08 4.58e+08 3.50e+08 3.50e+08 3.45e+08 3.37e+08 3.33e+08 3.25e+08 2.60e+08 2.58e+08 ...
##  $ Budget             : num  1.50e+08 2.50e+08 5.88e+07 1.90e+08 1.75e+08 1.25e+08 1.70e+08 7.40e+07 1.70e+08 6.00e+07 ...
##  $ Screens            : num  4274 4276 3555 4004 3946 ...
##  $ Sequel             : num  4 2 1 7 1 3 1 2 2 1 ...
##  $ Sentiment          : num  1 21 4 -17 2 2 0 -2 5 -2 ...
##  $ Views              : num  9143740 10366624 3927600 59056 1438926 ...
##  $ Likes              : num  34746 31552 13143 330 4632 ...
##  $ Dislikes           : num  1074 989 573 8 262 ...
##  $ Comments           : num  5107 3843 3134 39 496 ...
##  $ Aggregate.Followers: num  6180000 10070000 24300 11890000 232000 ...
# The skim() function provides a more detailed summary, including missing values,
# quantiles, and a small histogram for numeric variables.
skim(data_raw)
Data summary
Name data_raw
Number of rows 231
Number of columns 14
_______________________
Column type frequency:
character 1
numeric 13
________________________
Group variables None

Variable type: character

skim_variable n_missing complete_rate min max empty n_unique whitespace
Movie 0 1 3 41 0 231 0

Variable type: numeric

skim_variable n_missing complete_rate mean sd p0 p25 p50 p75 p100 hist
Year 0 1.00 2014.29 0.46 2014.0 2014.0 2014.0 2015.0 2015.0 ▇▁▁▁▃
Ratings 0 1.00 6.44 0.99 3.1 5.8 6.5 7.1 8.7 ▁▂▇▇▂
Genre 0 1.00 5.36 4.14 1.0 1.0 3.0 8.0 15.0 ▇▁▅▂▁
Gross 0 1.00 68066033.20 88902891.22 2470.0 10300000.0 37400000.0 89350000.0 643000000.0 ▇▁▁▁▁
Budget 1 1.00 47921730.05 54288246.07 70000.0 9000000.0 28000000.0 65000000.0 250000000.0 ▇▂▁▁▁
Screens 10 0.96 2209.24 1463.77 2.0 449.0 2777.0 3372.0 4324.0 ▇▁▂▇▅
Sequel 0 1.00 1.36 0.97 1.0 1.0 1.0 1.0 7.0 ▇▁▁▁▁
Sentiment 0 1.00 2.81 7.00 -38.0 0.0 0.0 5.5 29.0 ▁▁▇▃▁
Views 0 1.00 3712851.29 4511104.24 698.0 623302.0 2409338.0 5217379.5 32626778.0 ▇▂▁▁▁
Likes 0 1.00 12732.54 28825.48 1.0 1776.5 6096.0 15247.5 370552.0 ▇▁▁▁▁
Dislikes 0 1.00 679.05 1243.93 0.0 105.5 341.0 697.5 13960.0 ▇▁▁▁▁
Comments 0 1.00 1825.70 3571.04 0.0 248.5 837.0 2137.0 38363.0 ▇▁▁▁▁
Aggregate.Followers 35 0.85 3038193.45 4886278.07 1066.0 183025.0 1052600.0 3694500.0 31030000.0 ▇▁▁▁▁

Interpretation: The summaries reveal that the dataset contains numerical and character (Movie) variables. Several variables, notably Screens and Aggregate.Followers, contain missing values (n_missing > 0). Many of the numerical predictors like Gross, Budget, Views, and Likes are highly right-skewed, which is evident from the mean being much larger than the median and the shape of the inline histograms. This skewness will likely violate the assumptions of linear regression and may require transformation.

Missing Data Analysis

A thorough analysis of missing data is required to choose an appropriate handling strategy. We first visualize the extent and pattern of missingness.

# Create a summary table of missing values per variable.
miss_var_summary(data_raw)
## # A tibble: 14 × 3
##    variable            n_miss pct_miss
##    <chr>                <int>    <num>
##  1 Aggregate.Followers     35   15.2  
##  2 Screens                 10    4.33 
##  3 Budget                   1    0.433
##  4 Movie                    0    0    
##  5 Year                     0    0    
##  6 Ratings                  0    0    
##  7 Genre                    0    0    
##  8 Gross                    0    0    
##  9 Sequel                   0    0    
## 10 Sentiment                0    0    
## 11 Views                    0    0    
## 12 Likes                    0    0    
## 13 Dislikes                 0    0    
## 14 Comments                 0    0
# The vis_miss plot provides a heatmap-like overview of missing data locations.
vis_miss(data_raw, warn_large_data = FALSE)

# The aggr plot from the VIM package shows the amount of missingness
# for each variable and for combinations of variables.
aggr(data_raw, prop = TRUE, numbers = TRUE)

Interpretation: Aggregate.Followers has the highest percentage of missing data at 15.2%, followed by Screens at 4.3%, and Budget at 0.433%. The aggr plot shows that most missingness occurs in Aggregate.Followers alone. To select the correct imputation method, we need to understand the missing data mechanism.

Investigating the Missing Data Mechanism

We test whether the data is Missing Completely at Random (MCAR) or if the probability of a value being missing is related to other observed variables (Missing at Random - MAR).

First, we use logistic regression to predict the missingness of Aggregate.Followers based on other variables. If other variables can predict missingness, this is evidence for MAR.

Suppose the missingness of Aggregate.Followers is based on Views, Likes, Budget, Screens, and Ratings.

# Create an indicator variable: 1 if Aggregate.Followers is missing, 0 otherwise.
data_mar_test <- data_raw %>%
  mutate(missing_followers = ifelse(is.na(Aggregate.Followers), 1, 0))

# Fit a logistic regression model.
logit_model <- glm(
  missing_followers ~ Views + Likes + Budget + Screens + Ratings,
  data = data_mar_test,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)
summary(logit_model)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = missing_followers ~ Views + Likes + Budget + Screens + 
##     Ratings, family = binomial(link = "logit"), data = data_mar_test)
## 
## Coefficients:
##               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
## (Intercept) -4.713e+00  1.617e+00  -2.915  0.00356 **
## Views        8.750e-08  1.082e-07   0.808  0.41885   
## Likes       -3.422e-05  3.900e-05  -0.877  0.38031   
## Budget      -1.568e-08  7.271e-09  -2.157  0.03104 * 
## Screens     -8.423e-05  1.764e-04  -0.477  0.63309   
## Ratings      5.742e-01  2.373e-01   2.420  0.01553 * 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 185.99  on 219  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 167.68  on 214  degrees of freedom
##   (11 observations deleted due to missingness)
## AIC: 179.68
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6

Interpretation: The logistic regression model was fit to determine if the probability of Aggregate.Followers being missing is systematically related to other observed variables in the dataset. The results provide key insights into the missing data mechanism:

  • Significant Predictors: Two variables were found to be statistically significant predictors of missingness: Budget (p = 0.031) and Ratings (p = 0.016).

  • Effect of Budget: The coefficient for Budget is negative (-1.568e-08). This indicates that for every unit increase in a movie’s budget, the log-odds of its follower data being missing decrease. In practical terms, movies with lower budgets are significantly more likely to have missing Aggregate.Followers data.

  • Effect of Ratings: The coefficient for Ratings is positive (0.574). This implies that movies with higher ratings are also more likely to have their follower data missing. This might seem counter-intuitive but could reflect a pattern where niche or art-house films receive high critical ratings but lack the broad social media presence that is typically tracked.

Since the likelihood of a value being missing in Aggregate.Followers is clearly dependent on at least two other observed variables, we have strong evidence to reject the assumption that the data is Missing Completely at Random (MCAR).

=> This result strongly supports the Missing at Random (MAR) assumption, which means that a sophisticated imputation method that can model these relationships is appropriate.

We can further support this with t-tests, comparing the means of key variables for observations with and without missing followers.

# T-test for Budget
t.test(Budget ~ missing_followers, data = data_mar_test)
## 
##  Welch Two Sample t-test
## 
## data:  Budget by missing_followers
## t = 4.3501, df = 89.681, p-value = 3.591e-05
## alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group 0 and group 1 is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  15015925 40262286
## sample estimates:
## mean in group 0 mean in group 1 
##        52127681        24488575
# T-test for Likes
t.test(Likes ~ missing_followers, data = data_mar_test)
## 
##  Welch Two Sample t-test
## 
## data:  Likes by missing_followers
## t = 1.9168, df = 166.46, p-value = 0.05698
## alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group 0 and group 1 is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##   -159.8292 10810.0598
## sample estimates:
## mean in group 0 mean in group 1 
##       13539.372        8214.257

Interpretation: The t-test for Budget shows a highly significant difference in means (p < 0.001), confirming that movies with missing follower data have a much lower average budget. The test for Likes is marginally significant (p=0.057), suggesting a similar trend. This collective evidence strongly points to a MAR mechanism.

Finally, we perform Little’s MCAR test, where the null hypothesis is that data is MCAR.

# Refined subset for MCAR
numeric_data <- data_raw %>%
  select(where(is.numeric)) %>%
  select(-Genre, -Sequel, -Sentiment, -Year)

near_zero_var <- nearZeroVar(numeric_data)
if (length(near_zero_var) > 0) numeric_data <- numeric_data[, -near_zero_var]

numeric_data_scaled <- scale(numeric_data)

mcar_test_result <- mcar_test(as.data.frame(numeric_data_scaled))
print(mcar_test_result)
## # A tibble: 1 × 4
##   statistic    df p.value missing.patterns
##       <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>            <int>
## 1      45.2    31  0.0482                5
# Missing correlations
num_vars_with_miss <- data_raw %>%
  select(where(is.numeric))
cor_matrix_miss <- cor(num_vars_with_miss, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
corrplot(cor_matrix_miss, method = "circle", tl.cex = 0.8)

Interpretation: The p-value (0.048) from Little’s test is less than 0.05, leading us to reject the null hypothesis. This formally confirms that the data is not Missing Completely at Random.

Data Imputation

Given the strong evidence for MAR, we can use a sophisticated imputation method that leverages relationships between variables. We choose missForest, which uses a random forest algorithm to predict and fill in missing values. It can handle mixed data types and complex interactions effectively.

df <- data_raw[rowSums(is.na(data_raw)) != ncol(data_raw), ]

df_impute <- df[, -which(names(df) == "Movie")]

cols_to_factor <- c("Genre", "Year")
df_impute[cols_to_factor] <- lapply(df_impute[cols_to_factor], factor)

set.seed(123)
result_imputation <- missForest(df_impute, verbose = TRUE, ntree = 100)
##   missForest iteration 1 in progress...done!
##     estimated error(s): 0.2450955 0 
##     difference(s): 0.0004215305 0 
##     time: 0.12 seconds
## 
##   missForest iteration 2 in progress...done!
##     estimated error(s): 0.2484268 0 
##     difference(s): 1.932914e-06 0 
##     time: 0.14 seconds
## 
##   missForest iteration 3 in progress...done!
##     estimated error(s): 0.2456973 0 
##     difference(s): 2.44642e-06 0 
##     time: 0.13 seconds
df_completed <- result_imputation$ximp
df_completed$Aggregate.Followers <- round(df_completed$Aggregate.Followers)

sapply(df_completed, function(x) sum(is.na(x)))
##                Year             Ratings               Genre               Gross 
##                   0                   0                   0                   0 
##              Budget             Screens              Sequel           Sentiment 
##                   0                   0                   0                   0 
##               Views               Likes            Dislikes            Comments 
##                   0                   0                   0                   0 
## Aggregate.Followers 
##                   0
print(result_imputation$OOBerror)
##     NRMSE       PFC 
## 0.2484268 0.0000000
neg_checks <- sapply(c("Budget", "Gross", "Aggregate.Followers"), function(col) sum(df_completed[[col]] < 0))
print(neg_checks)
##              Budget               Gross Aggregate.Followers 
##                   0                   0                   0

Interpretation: The imputation process has completed, and the dataset now has no missing values. The low OOB error suggests that the imputations are of good quality. We can now proceed with the imputed dataset.

Post-Imputation Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

Before modeling, we perform a final EDA on the complete data to understand relationships and confirm data characteristics.

complete_data <- df_completed

ggplot() +
  geom_density(data = data_raw, aes(x = Aggregate.Followers), color = "blue", na.rm = TRUE) +
  geom_density(data = complete_data, aes(x = Aggregate.Followers), color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Aggregate Followers: Original (blue) vs Imputed (red)") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(complete_data, aes(y = Gross)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal() +
  ggtitle("Boxplot of Gross Revenue")

library(GGally)
num_vars <- dplyr::select(complete_data, where(is.numeric))
ggpairs(num_vars, title = "Pairwise Scatterplots and Correlations")

Interpretation: The density plot shows that the imputation has preserved the overall distribution of Aggregate.Followers, which is a good sign. The boxplot for Gross confirms the severe right-skewness and presence of high-end outliers, reinforcing the need for transformation during the modeling phase.

b) Split the dataset

We partition the complete dataset into a training set (80%) for model building and a validation set (20%) for evaluating the final model’s performance on unseen data.

# Set a seed to ensure the split is reproducible.

set.seed(50)

# Create an index for the training set.
train_index <- sample(1:nrow(complete_data), size = 0.8 * nrow(complete_data))

# Create the training and validation sets.
train <- complete_data[train_index, ]
valid <- complete_data[-train_index, ]

# Print the dimensions of the resulting sets.
cat("Training set rows:", nrow(train), "\n")
## Training set rows: 184
cat("Validation set rows:", nrow(valid), "\n")
## Validation set rows: 47

c) Model selection

This section details the iterative process of selecting the best linear regression model to explain Gross. The process includes feature selection, assumption diagnostics, outlier handling, and variable transformations.

Initial Full Model

We begin by fitting a model that includes all potential predictors to serve as a starting point.

full_model <- lm(Gross ~ ., data = train)
summary(full_model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Gross ~ ., data = train)
## 
## Residuals:
##        Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
## -112259452  -30324174   -4394688   19041975  416247723 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                       Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         -1.310e+08  3.657e+07  -3.582 0.000450 ***
## Year2015            -1.322e+07  1.132e+07  -1.168 0.244569    
## Ratings              1.907e+07  5.308e+06   3.592 0.000435 ***
## Genre2              -3.099e+07  2.129e+07  -1.455 0.147556    
## Genre3              -1.662e+07  1.393e+07  -1.193 0.234611    
## Genre4              -3.419e+07  5.956e+07  -0.574 0.566714    
## Genre6              -3.338e+07  3.496e+07  -0.955 0.341097    
## Genre7               1.835e+07  4.322e+07   0.425 0.671699    
## Genre8              -1.439e+07  1.447e+07  -0.994 0.321479    
## Genre9              -1.478e+07  2.077e+07  -0.711 0.477802    
## Genre10             -3.188e+07  2.125e+07  -1.500 0.135519    
## Genre12             -1.001e+07  2.126e+07  -0.471 0.638530    
## Genre15              1.258e+07  2.432e+07   0.517 0.605760    
## Budget               6.944e-01  1.245e-01   5.577    1e-07 ***
## Screens              1.396e+04  4.090e+03   3.413 0.000812 ***
## Sequel               1.360e+07  5.454e+06   2.494 0.013638 *  
## Sentiment           -4.649e+03  6.332e+05  -0.007 0.994151    
## Views               -1.855e+00  1.803e+00  -1.029 0.304892    
## Likes                6.158e+02  6.468e+02   0.952 0.342457    
## Dislikes             1.304e+04  5.774e+03   2.258 0.025296 *  
## Comments            -4.026e+03  4.699e+03  -0.857 0.392848    
## Aggregate.Followers  2.535e+00  1.020e+00   2.486 0.013941 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 57220000 on 162 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6513, Adjusted R-squared:  0.6061 
## F-statistic: 14.41 on 21 and 162 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
avPlots(full_model, id = FALSE)

Interpretation:

  • Overall Fit: The model is statistically significant (F-statistic p-value < 0.001) and explains about 61% of the variance in Gross (R-squared = 0.6061).

  • Key Predictors: Budget, Screens, Ratings, and Aggregate.Followers are highly significant predictors.

Conclusion: The model is too complex. Many variables (e.g., Year, most Genre types, Sentiment) are not significant, which justifies using feature selection to create a simpler, more effective model.

Feature Selection

The initial full_model, while having a high R-squared, was overly complex and contained many statistically insignificant variables. The goal of this stage is to simplify the model by retaining only the most important predictors, creating a model that is both powerful in its explanatory ability and parsimonious for better interpretation. We will use a combination of two methods: stepwise selection and best subset selection.

Backward Stepwise Selection

This method begins with the full model and systematically removes the least significant variable at each step until no further improvement can be made according to a specific criterion. We will use two common criteria, AIC and BIC.

  • AIC (Akaike Information Criterion): Balances model fit and complexity.

  • BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion): Similar to AIC but imposes a stronger penalty for model complexity, thus tending to select smaller models.

# Backward selection using AIC (Akaike Information Criterion)
step_aic <- step(full_model, direction = "backward", trace = 0)
cat("--- Model selected by AIC --- \n")
## --- Model selected by AIC ---
summary(step_aic)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Gross ~ Ratings + Budget + Screens + Sequel + Dislikes + 
##     Aggregate.Followers, data = train)
## 
## Residuals:
##        Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
## -121927602  -24326357   -3039484   18857587  413078821 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                       Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         -1.245e+08  3.048e+07  -4.085 6.68e-05 ***
## Ratings              1.546e+07  4.560e+06   3.390 0.000861 ***
## Budget               7.535e-01  1.066e-01   7.067 3.50e-11 ***
## Screens              1.305e+04  3.665e+03   3.561 0.000475 ***
## Sequel               1.262e+07  4.992e+06   2.528 0.012335 *  
## Dislikes             6.621e+03  3.437e+03   1.926 0.055649 .  
## Aggregate.Followers  2.456e+00  9.535e-01   2.576 0.010820 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 56120000 on 177 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6334, Adjusted R-squared:  0.621 
## F-statistic: 50.98 on 6 and 177 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
# Backward selection using BIC (k = log(n))
n <- nrow(train)
step_bic <- step(full_model, direction = "backward", k = log(n), trace = 0)
cat("\n--- Model selected by BIC --- \n")
## 
## --- Model selected by BIC ---
summary(step_bic)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Gross ~ Ratings + Budget + Screens + Sequel + Aggregate.Followers, 
##     data = train)
## 
## Residuals:
##        Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
## -128549728  -27482739   -1764488   16828321  416248094 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                       Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         -1.074e+08  2.938e+07  -3.655 0.000338 ***
## Ratings              1.313e+07  4.430e+06   2.964 0.003456 ** 
## Budget               7.615e-01  1.074e-01   7.093 2.98e-11 ***
## Screens              1.465e+04  3.596e+03   4.074 6.94e-05 ***
## Sequel               1.141e+07  4.990e+06   2.286 0.023410 *  
## Aggregate.Followers  2.558e+00  9.592e-01   2.666 0.008373 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 56550000 on 178 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6258, Adjusted R-squared:  0.6152 
## F-statistic: 59.53 on 5 and 178 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Interpretation:

  • AIC Model: Retained 6 predictor variables (Ratings, Budget, Screens, Sequel, Dislikes, Aggregate.Followers).

  • BIC Model: Retained only 5 predictor variables (Ratings, Budget, Screens, Sequel, Aggregate.Followers).

As expected, the BIC criterion has selected a considerably more parsimonious model. All variables in the BIC model are highly statistically significant (p-value < 0.05). Therefore, we will consider these 6 variables as the strongest “candidates” for a more in-depth analysis in the next step.

Best Subset Selection

This method exhaustively evaluates all possible models that can be built from the set of 6 candidate variables (the best 1-variable model, the best 2-variable model, etc.) and assesses them using multiple criteria.

csm_models <- regsubsets(Gross ~ Ratings + Budget + Screens + Sequel + Dislikes + Aggregate.Followers,
                         data = train, nvmax = 6)
summary_csm <- summary(csm_models)

# Display the variables included in the best model for each size
cat("--- Variables in Each Best Model ---\n")
## --- Variables in Each Best Model ---
print(summary_csm$which)
##   (Intercept) Ratings Budget Screens Sequel Dislikes Aggregate.Followers
## 1        TRUE   FALSE   TRUE   FALSE  FALSE    FALSE               FALSE
## 2        TRUE   FALSE   TRUE    TRUE  FALSE    FALSE               FALSE
## 3        TRUE   FALSE   TRUE    TRUE  FALSE    FALSE                TRUE
## 4        TRUE    TRUE   TRUE    TRUE  FALSE    FALSE                TRUE
## 5        TRUE    TRUE   TRUE    TRUE   TRUE    FALSE                TRUE
## 6        TRUE    TRUE   TRUE    TRUE   TRUE     TRUE                TRUE
num_models_found <- length(summary_csm$rsq)

# Tạo data frame một cách linh hoạt
metrics_df <- data.frame(
  Num_Vars = 1:num_models_found,
  R_Squared = summary_csm$rsq,
  Adj_R_Squared = summary_csm$adjr2,
  Mallows_Cp = summary_csm$cp,
  BIC = summary_csm$bic
)

cat("\n--- Comparison of Model Selection Criteria ---\n")
## 
## --- Comparison of Model Selection Criteria ---
print(metrics_df)
##   Num_Vars R_Squared Adj_R_Squared Mallows_Cp       BIC
## 1        1 0.5347906     0.5322345  44.636185 -130.3794
## 2        2 0.5707160     0.5659726  29.288845 -139.9523
## 3        3 0.5968354     0.5901160  18.676564 -146.2878
## 4        4 0.6147660     0.6061574  12.018407 -149.4437
## 5        5 0.6257568     0.6152443   8.711262 -149.5547
## 6        6 0.6334426     0.6210169   7.000000 -148.1579

Interpretation:

The table evaluates the trade-off between model fit and complexity for the best models of each size from 1 to 6 variables.

  • Adjusted R-Squared: This metric increases with each added variable, peaking at 0.62 for the 6-variable model, indicating it provides the best fit when penalizing for complexity.

  • Mallows’ Cp: The goal is a model where Cp is small and close to the number of parameters (p = number of variables + 1). The 6-variable model is optimal, as its Cp of 7.0 is exactly equal to p (6+1=7).

  • BIC: This criterion, which most strongly penalizes complexity, reaches its minimum (best) value of -149.5 for the 5-variable model.

=> Conclusion: Given that two of the three key metrics are optimal for the 6-variable model, it represents a strong choice balancing fit and predictive power.

Diagnostics of the Selected Model

We must check if our selected model satisfies the core assumptions of linear regression: linearity, normality of residuals, homoscedasticity (constant variance), and absence of severe multicollinearity.

# Define the selected model based on BIC results.
selected_model <- lm(Gross ~ Ratings + Budget + Screens + Sequel + Dislikes + Aggregate.Followers, data = train)
summary(selected_model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Gross ~ Ratings + Budget + Screens + Sequel + Dislikes + 
##     Aggregate.Followers, data = train)
## 
## Residuals:
##        Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
## -121927602  -24326357   -3039484   18857587  413078821 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                       Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         -1.245e+08  3.048e+07  -4.085 6.68e-05 ***
## Ratings              1.546e+07  4.560e+06   3.390 0.000861 ***
## Budget               7.535e-01  1.066e-01   7.067 3.50e-11 ***
## Screens              1.305e+04  3.665e+03   3.561 0.000475 ***
## Sequel               1.262e+07  4.992e+06   2.528 0.012335 *  
## Dislikes             6.621e+03  3.437e+03   1.926 0.055649 .  
## Aggregate.Followers  2.456e+00  9.535e-01   2.576 0.010820 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 56120000 on 177 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6334, Adjusted R-squared:  0.621 
## F-statistic: 50.98 on 6 and 177 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
# 1. Residuals vs. Fitted plot (for linearity and homoscedasticity)
# 2. Normal Q-Q plot (for normality of residuals)
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(selected_model, which = 1)
plot(selected_model, which = 2)

# Shapiro-Wilk test for normality
shapiro.test(residuals(selected_model))
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  residuals(selected_model)
## W = 0.79427, p-value = 8.025e-15
# Breusch-Pagan test for homoscedasticity (null: constant variance)
bptest(selected_model)
## 
##  studentized Breusch-Pagan test
## 
## data:  selected_model
## BP = 15.684, df = 6, p-value = 0.01555
# Variance Inflation Factors (VIF) for multicollinearity (VIF > 5-10 indicates issues)
vif(selected_model)
##             Ratings              Budget             Screens              Sequel 
##            1.194940            2.030247            1.671737            1.438906 
##            Dislikes Aggregate.Followers 
##            1.176793            1.134619

Interpretation:

  • Residuals vs. Fitted Plot: The plot shows a distinct funnel shape, where the variance of the residuals increases as the fitted values increase. This is a clear sign of heteroscedasticity.

  • Normal Q-Q Plot: The residuals deviate heavily from the theoretical line, especially at the tails, indicating they are not normally distributed.

  • Breusch-Pagan Test: The p-value is small (< 0.05), so we reject the null hypothesis and confirm the presence of heteroscedasticity.

  • VIF: All VIF values are low (<= 2), indicating that multicollinearity is not an issue in this model.

The model violates key assumptions and requires correction.

Outlier Detection and Removal

Influential outliers can disproportionately affect model coefficients and diagnostics. We use an influence plot to identify them.

# The influence plot shows Cook's distance, leverage, and studentized residuals.
influenceIndexPlot(selected_model, id = TRUE)

Interpretation:

Looking at Cook’s distance, the candidate points are 1 and 29. For studentized residuals, the potential outliers is point 1. From the hat values, the potential high leverage point is 29. By removing the points from the original dataset and constructing another summary table, we are able to determine if the points are influential points.

Test for Outlier Removal

After removing the outliers, we repeat the feature selection process on the cleaned data.

# Remove the identified outlier rows.
outlier_rows <- c("1", "29")
train_cleaned <- train[!rownames(train) %in% outlier_rows, ]

# Refit the 6-variable model on the cleaned data.
refit_model <- lm(Gross ~ Ratings + Budget + Screens + Sequel + Dislikes + Aggregate.Followers, data = train_cleaned)
summary(refit_model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Gross ~ Ratings + Budget + Screens + Sequel + Dislikes + 
##     Aggregate.Followers, data = train_cleaned)
## 
## Residuals:
##        Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
## -124992842  -23362947   -1493183   16553533  257012206 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                       Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         -1.024e+08  2.530e+07  -4.048 7.75e-05 ***
## Ratings              1.359e+07  3.761e+06   3.612 0.000396 ***
## Budget               7.541e-01  8.774e-02   8.595 4.52e-15 ***
## Screens              1.279e+04  3.041e+03   4.206 4.15e-05 ***
## Sequel               6.136e+06  4.178e+06   1.469 0.143701    
## Dislikes             3.611e+02  4.322e+03   0.084 0.933504    
## Aggregate.Followers  2.725e+00  7.880e-01   3.458 0.000683 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 46120000 on 175 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6839, Adjusted R-squared:  0.6731 
## F-statistic: 63.12 on 6 and 175 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Interpretation: From our original summary table constructed with all the points in our dataset, we obtained residual standard error amount of 56120000, R-squared value of 0.6334 and adjusted R-squared value 0.621. This means that 62.1% of variability in gross income is explained by our current regression model. From our new summary table, the residual standard error is 46120000 which is significantly lower than our previous residual value. This means that the errors of prediction in our model is improved. The new R-squared value is 0.6839 and the adjusted R-squared value is 0.6731. This means that 67.31% of the variability in gross income is explained by our current regression model which is better than the original one. Through this method, we are able to determine that the points 1 and 29 are indeed influential points and should be removed from our model.

Re-selection After Outlier Removal

# Re-run backward selection using AIC on the cleaned data.
step_aic_clean <- step(refit_model, direction = "backward", trace = 0)
summary(step_aic_clean)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Gross ~ Ratings + Budget + Screens + Sequel + Aggregate.Followers, 
##     data = train_cleaned)
## 
## Residuals:
##        Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
## -125263821  -23518450   -1482209   16436858  256962117 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                       Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         -1.018e+08  2.422e+07  -4.204 4.17e-05 ***
## Ratings              1.351e+07  3.650e+06   3.702 0.000286 ***
## Budget               7.546e-01  8.731e-02   8.643 3.28e-15 ***
## Screens              1.285e+04  2.938e+03   4.375 2.08e-05 ***
## Sequel               6.075e+06  4.103e+06   1.481 0.140492    
## Aggregate.Followers  2.732e+00  7.805e-01   3.501 0.000588 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 45990000 on 176 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6839, Adjusted R-squared:  0.675 
## F-statistic: 76.17 on 5 and 176 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
# Final model post re-selection
final_model <- step_aic_clean

# Final Model Diagnostics
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(final_model, which = 1)
plot(final_model, which = 2)

shapiro.test(residuals(final_model))
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  residuals(final_model)
## W = 0.9037, p-value = 1.663e-09
bptest(final_model)
## 
##  studentized Breusch-Pagan test
## 
## data:  final_model
## BP = 23.981, df = 5, p-value = 0.0002189
vif(final_model)
##             Ratings              Budget             Screens              Sequel 
##            1.103999            1.989049            1.571674            1.391939 
## Aggregate.Followers 
##            1.128037

Interpretation:

  • After removing the outliers and through AIC, Dislikes are no longer significant. The new, more parsimonious model includes only four predictors: Ratings, Budget, Screens, Sequel and Aggregate.Followers.

  • Residuals improved but still show mild heteroscedasticity and non-normality (Shapiro p<0.001). VIF low. Proceed to transformations for better adherence.

Variable Transformations

Predictor Transformations: Power Transform for Non-Categorical Variables

This new 5-variable model still suffers from the same assumption violations due to data skewness. We now apply transformations to correct this.

trans_predictors <- powerTransform(cbind(Ratings, Budget, Screens, Sequel, Aggregate.Followers) ~ 1, data = train_cleaned, family = "bcPower")
summary(trans_predictors)
## bcPower Transformations to Multinormality 
##                     Est Power Rounded Pwr Wald Lwr Bnd Wald Upr Bnd
## Ratings                1.8819        2.00       1.1207       2.6432
## Budget                 0.2137        0.21       0.1340       0.2933
## Screens                0.6341        0.63       0.5135       0.7547
## Sequel                -4.6427       -4.64      -5.4525      -3.8329
## Aggregate.Followers    0.1910        0.19       0.1265       0.2554
## 
## Likelihood ratio test that transformation parameters are equal to 0
##  (all log transformations)
##                                    LRT df       pval
## LR test, lambda = (0 0 0 0 0) 494.9887  5 < 2.22e-16
## 
## Likelihood ratio test that no transformations are needed
##                                    LRT df       pval
## LR test, lambda = (1 1 1 1 1) 1163.982  5 < 2.22e-16

Interpretation

This powerTransform output suggests the optimal power (lambda, λ) to make the predictor distributions more symmetric and linearize their relationships with the response.

  • Ratings: The 95% confidence interval for the estimated power [1.12, 2.64] includes 2.0.

  • Budget & Aggregate.Followers: Both variables require a strong transformation. Their estimated powers are 0.21 and 0.19, respectively. Their confidence intervals are far from 1.0 (no transformation) and 0 (log transformation). A square-root (λ=0.5) or fourth-root (λ=0.25) transformation is strongly recommended to correct their severe right-skew.

  • Screens: The estimated power of 0.63 with a confidence interval of [0.51, 0.75] suggests that a mild transformation, weaker than a square root, is beneficial.

  • Sequel: The function returned an estimate of -4.64. This extreme and nonsensical value indicates that powerTransform is inappropriate for this variable, as Sequel is a count/ordinal variable, not a continuous one.

=> Conclusion: Based on this, applying a square-root transformation to Budget and Aggregate.Followers, and square transformation to Ratings while leaving Screens, and Sequel untransformed is a practical and statistically justified approach to improve the model.

Box-Cox for Gross

We use the Box-Cox procedure to find the optimal power transformation for the response variable, Gross.

# First, we'll transform skewed predictors. 'powerTransform' suggests sqrt for Budget and Followers.
transformed_predictors_model <- lm(Gross ~ I(Ratings**(2.0)) + sqrt(Budget) + Screens + Sequel + sqrt(Aggregate.Followers), data = train_cleaned)

# Apply Box-Cox to this model to find the optimal transformation for Gross.
boxcox_result <- boxCox(transformed_predictors_model, lambda = seq(0, 0.5, by = 0.01))

lambda_optimal <- boxcox_result$x[which.max(boxcox_result$y)]
cat("Optimal lambda for Box-Cox transformation:", lambda_optimal, "\n")
## Optimal lambda for Box-Cox transformation: 0.3333333
# Compute 95% confidence interval for lambda
max_log_like <- max(boxcox_result$y)
ci_threshold <- max_log_like - 0.5 * qchisq(0.95, 1)
ci_lambdas <- boxcox_result$x[boxcox_result$y >= ci_threshold]
ci_lower <- min(ci_lambdas)
ci_upper <- max(ci_lambdas)
cat("95% Confidence Interval for lambda: [", ci_lower, ",", ci_upper, "]\n")
## 95% Confidence Interval for lambda: [ 0.2828283 , 0.3838384 ]

Interpretation: The optimal lambda is around 0.33. This value is very close to both 0 (log transform) and 0.5 (square root transform). For ease of interpretation and to maintain a more intuitive scale, we will use the square root transformation (lambda=0.5) for Gross.

Re-Check Assumptions for Final Transformed Model

# Fit model with chosen lambda=0.5 (sqrt(Gross)) for comparison/simplicity
transformed_response_chosen <- lm(sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings**(2.0)) + sqrt(Budget) + Screens + Sequel + sqrt(Aggregate.Followers),
                                  data = train_cleaned)
summary(transformed_response_chosen)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings^(2)) + sqrt(Budget) + Screens + 
##     Sequel + sqrt(Aggregate.Followers), data = train_cleaned)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -7972.5 -1631.6  -275.3  1608.7  9303.1 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)               -3.052e+03  7.203e+02  -4.237 3.64e-05 ***
## I(Ratings^(2))             6.935e+01  1.602e+01   4.328 2.52e-05 ***
## sqrt(Budget)               4.762e-01  7.379e-02   6.453 1.03e-09 ***
## Screens                    1.289e+00  1.689e-01   7.635 1.38e-12 ***
## Sequel                     4.651e+02  2.157e+02   2.156   0.0324 *  
## sqrt(Aggregate.Followers)  3.958e-01  1.847e-01   2.143   0.0335 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 2501 on 176 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.7148, Adjusted R-squared:  0.7067 
## F-statistic: 88.23 on 5 and 176 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
# Backward stepwise on chosen lambda model
step_chosen <- step(transformed_response_chosen, direction = "backward", trace = TRUE)
## Start:  AIC=2853.98
## sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings^(2)) + sqrt(Budget) + Screens + Sequel + 
##     sqrt(Aggregate.Followers)
## 
##                             Df Sum of Sq        RSS    AIC
## <none>                                   1100796346 2854.0
## - sqrt(Aggregate.Followers)  1  28728600 1129524947 2856.7
## - Sequel                     1  29079872 1129876219 2856.7
## - I(Ratings^(2))             1 117158976 1217955322 2870.4
## - sqrt(Budget)               1 260443693 1361240039 2890.6
## - Screens                    1 364637344 1465433691 2904.1
summary(step_chosen)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings^(2)) + sqrt(Budget) + Screens + 
##     Sequel + sqrt(Aggregate.Followers), data = train_cleaned)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -7972.5 -1631.6  -275.3  1608.7  9303.1 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)               -3.052e+03  7.203e+02  -4.237 3.64e-05 ***
## I(Ratings^(2))             6.935e+01  1.602e+01   4.328 2.52e-05 ***
## sqrt(Budget)               4.762e-01  7.379e-02   6.453 1.03e-09 ***
## Screens                    1.289e+00  1.689e-01   7.635 1.38e-12 ***
## Sequel                     4.651e+02  2.157e+02   2.156   0.0324 *  
## sqrt(Aggregate.Followers)  3.958e-01  1.847e-01   2.143   0.0335 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 2501 on 176 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.7148, Adjusted R-squared:  0.7067 
## F-statistic: 88.23 on 5 and 176 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
# Select final transformed model (e.g., from chosen lambda stepwise)
final_transformed_model <- step_chosen

# Set up plot grid
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))

# 1. Linearity & Homoscedasticity: Residuals vs. Fitted
plot(final_transformed_model, which = 1)
# Expected: Random scatter, no funnel shape (improved homoscedasticity)

# 2. Normality: Normal Q-Q Plot
plot(final_transformed_model, which = 2)
# Expected: Points closer to diagonal line

# 3. Scale-Location (alternative for heteroscedasticity)
plot(final_transformed_model, which = 3)

# 4. Residuals vs. Leverage (for outliers/influence)
plot(final_transformed_model, which = 5)

# Statistical tests
cat("\nShapiro-Wilk Test for Normality (Post-Transformation):\n")
## 
## Shapiro-Wilk Test for Normality (Post-Transformation):
print(shapiro.test(residuals(final_transformed_model))) # p-value = 0.1704
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  residuals(final_transformed_model)
## W = 0.98603, p-value = 0.06755
cat("\nBreusch-Pagan Test for Homoscedasticity (Post-Transformation):\n")
## 
## Breusch-Pagan Test for Homoscedasticity (Post-Transformation):
print(bptest(final_transformed_model)) # p-value = 0.2281
## 
##  studentized Breusch-Pagan test
## 
## data:  final_transformed_model
## BP = 9.9303, df = 5, p-value = 0.07723
# Additional: Non-constant variance score test (from car)
ncvTest(final_transformed_model) # p-value = 0.063692
## Non-constant Variance Score Test 
## Variance formula: ~ fitted.values 
## Chisquare = 7.229451, Df = 1, p = 0.0071717
cat("\nVIF for Multicollinearity (Post-Transformation):\n")
## 
## VIF for Multicollinearity (Post-Transformation):
print(vif(final_transformed_model))
##            I(Ratings^(2))              sqrt(Budget)                   Screens 
##                  1.118313                  2.102120                  1.756055 
##                    Sequel sqrt(Aggregate.Followers) 
##                  1.300856                  1.148369

Interpretation

  • Predictor transforms (sqrt Budget/Followers, and square for Ratings) and Box-Cox optimal lambda improve model (R²=0.7148).

  • Post-transformation diagnostics: Residuals more random, Q-Q closer to line (Shapiro p=0.06755 > 0.05, normality OK). BP p=0.07723 > 0.05 (homoscedasticity improved), though NCV p=0.007. Scale-location even; leverage low. VIF <= 2.1 => Assumptions satisfied.

Testing for Interaction Effects

A simple additive model may not capture all the dynamics. It is plausible that the effect of a good rating is amplified for movies with a large budget. We test for this interaction.

# Base transformed model (without interaction)
final_transformed_model <- lm(sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings**(2.0)) + sqrt(Budget) + Screens + Sequel + sqrt(Aggregate.Followers), data =  train_cleaned)

# Test interaction between sqrt(Budget) and Screens
interact_model_1 <- lm(sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings**(2.0)) + sqrt(Budget) * Screens + Sequel + sqrt(Aggregate.Followers),
                       data = train_cleaned)

# ANOVA to compare with base transformed model (null: no interaction)
anova(final_transformed_model, interact_model_1) 
## Analysis of Variance Table
## 
## Model 1: sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings^(2)) + sqrt(Budget) + Screens + Sequel + 
##     sqrt(Aggregate.Followers)
## Model 2: sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings^(2)) + sqrt(Budget) * Screens + Sequel + 
##     sqrt(Aggregate.Followers)
##   Res.Df        RSS Df Sum of Sq      F Pr(>F)
## 1    176 1100796346                           
## 2    175 1100563058  1    233289 0.0371 0.8475
# Interpretation: As p-value = 0.5962 > 0.05, no significant interaction between Budget and Screens—retain base model.

# Test interaction between Ratings and sqrt(Budget)
interact_model_2 <- lm(sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings**(2.0)) * sqrt(Budget) + Screens + Sequel + sqrt(Aggregate.Followers),
                       data = train_cleaned)

# ANOVA to compare with base transformed model
anova(final_transformed_model, interact_model_2)
## Analysis of Variance Table
## 
## Model 1: sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings^(2)) + sqrt(Budget) + Screens + Sequel + 
##     sqrt(Aggregate.Followers)
## Model 2: sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings^(2)) * sqrt(Budget) + Screens + Sequel + 
##     sqrt(Aggregate.Followers)
##   Res.Df        RSS Df Sum of Sq      F  Pr(>F)  
## 1    176 1100796346                              
## 2    175 1064086504  1  36709842 6.0373 0.01498 *
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Interpretation: The ANOVA test yields a highly significant p-value (p < 0.05). This provides strong evidence that the interaction between Ratings and sqrt(Budget) is meaningful and improves the model’s explanatory power. We will therefore adopt the interaction model as our final best model.

Final Best Model and Diagnostics

Here is the summary and final diagnostic check of our chosen model.

# Our final, best model
best_model <- interact_model_2
summary(best_model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings^(2)) * sqrt(Budget) + Screens + 
##     Sequel + sqrt(Aggregate.Followers), data = train_cleaned)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -7776.9 -1498.5  -260.6  1471.6  9229.9 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)                 -6.344e+02  1.213e+03  -0.523   0.6018    
## I(Ratings^(2))               1.728e+01  2.643e+01   0.654   0.5142    
## sqrt(Budget)                 6.565e-02  1.822e-01   0.360   0.7191    
## Screens                      1.248e+00  1.673e-01   7.457 3.95e-12 ***
## Sequel                       3.075e+02  2.222e+02   1.384   0.1681    
## sqrt(Aggregate.Followers)    4.113e-01  1.822e-01   2.258   0.0252 *  
## I(Ratings^(2)):sqrt(Budget)  9.497e-03  3.865e-03   2.457   0.0150 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 2466 on 175 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.7243, Adjusted R-squared:  0.7149 
## F-statistic: 76.63 on 6 and 175 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
# Visualize the significant interaction effect
plot(effect("I(Ratings^(2)):sqrt(Budget)", best_model), multiline = TRUE, ci.style = "bands")

# Final check of model assumptions
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(best_model, which = 1) # Residuals vs. Fitted
plot(best_model, which = 2) # Normal Q-Q

bptest(best_model)          # Breusch-Pagan Test
## 
##  studentized Breusch-Pagan test
## 
## data:  best_model
## BP = 9.0875, df = 6, p-value = 0.1687

Interpretation of Final Model:

  • Model Fit: The Adjusted R-squared is 0.7149, indicating the model explains about 71.5% of the variance in sqrt(Gross).

  • Assumption Checks: The diagnostic plots are now much improved. The Residuals vs. Fitted plot shows a much more random scatter, and the Q-Q plot shows residuals are very close to the normality line. The Breusch-Pagan test p-value is 0.1687, so we fail to reject the null hypothesis of homoscedasticity. Our final model now satisfies the key assumptions of linear regression.

d) Prediction

Using the validated best_model, we predict Gross revenue for the unseen validation set. Since the model predicts sqrt(Gross), we must back-transform the predictions by squaring them.

# Predict sqrt(Gross) on the validation set.
predictions_sqrt <- predict(best_model, newdata = valid)

# Back-transform the predictions to the original scale (Gross).
predictions_original <- predictions_sqrt^2

# Create a data frame for comparison.
results_df <- data.frame(
  Actual_Gross = valid$Gross,
  Predicted_Gross = predictions_original
)
head(results_df)
##    Actual_Gross Predicted_Gross
## 5      3.45e+08       263533488
## 8      3.25e+08       108771154
## 22     1.88e+08       258028814
## 25     1.77e+08       137315745
## 27     1.69e+08       192053326
## 34     1.50e+08        63259105

e) Comparison and conclusion

We evaluate the model’s predictive accuracy on the validation set using standard metrics and visualization. To ensure our model’s performance is stable, we also conduct a 10-fold repeated cross-validation.

Validation Set Performance

# Calculate performance metrics.
actuals <- results_df$Actual_Gross
predictions <- results_df$Predicted_Gross

mae <- mean(abs(predictions - actuals))
rmse <- sqrt(mean((predictions - actuals)^2))
r2 <- cor(predictions, actuals)^2

# Print the metrics.
cat("--- Validation Set Metrics ---\n")
## --- Validation Set Metrics ---
cat("Mean Absolute Error (MAE):", format(mae, big.mark = ","), "\n")
## Mean Absolute Error (MAE): 32,841,452
cat("Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE):", format(rmse, big.mark = ","), "\n")
## Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): 50,039,403
cat("R-squared (R²):", r2, "\n")
## R-squared (R²): 0.6118674
# Visualize Actual vs. Predicted values.
ggplot(results_df, aes(x = Actual_Gross, y = Predicted_Gross)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, color = "blue") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(title = "Actual vs. Predicted Gross (Validation Set)", x = "Actual Gross", y = "Predicted Gross") +
  theme_minimal()

Interpretation:

We evaluated the final model’s performance on the validation set—data that the model has never seen before.

  • On the validation set, the model explains approximately 61.2% of the variance in Gross, indicating a reasonably good fit.

  • The MAE indicates that the model’s predictions are, on average, off by about $32.8 million.

  • The RMSE, which penalizes larger errors more heavily, is $50 million, suggesting that the model occasionally makes significant prediction errors.

While this performance is acceptable, the R-squared of 61.2% is noticeably lower than what was observed on the training data. This raises a critical question:

Is this 61.2% performance a reliable estimate of how the model will perform on new data in general? Or is it simply the result of one potentially “unlucky” data split?

A single validation set might not be perfectly representative of the overall data distribution, and its results can be sensitive to random chance. To obtain a more robust and stable estimate of the model’s true performance, a more rigorous evaluation method is needed.

Therefore, the next logical step is to perform Cross-Validation. By training and testing the model on multiple different subsets of the data, cross-validation will provide a more reliable performance estimate that is less biased by the randomness of a single train-test split.

Repeated Cross-Validation Performance

Cross-validation provides a more robust estimate of model performance by repeatedly splitting the data into folds and averaging the results.

# Combine cleaned training and validation data for a full CV dataset.
full_data_for_cv <- rbind(train_cleaned, valid)

# Define control for 10-fold repeated CV (5 repeats).
cv_control_repeated <- trainControl(method = "repeatedcv", number = 10, repeats = 5)

# Train the model using repeated CV.
set.seed(123)
cv_model_repeated <- train(
  sqrt(Gross) ~ I(Ratings^(2)) * sqrt(Budget) + Screens + Sequel + sqrt(Aggregate.Followers),
  data = full_data_for_cv,
  method = "lm",
  trControl = cv_control_repeated
)
print(cv_model_repeated)
## Linear Regression 
## 
## 229 samples
##   5 predictor
## 
## No pre-processing
## Resampling: Cross-Validated (10 fold, repeated 5 times) 
## Summary of sample sizes: 205, 205, 205, 207, 206, 208, ... 
## Resampling results:
## 
##   RMSE     Rsquared  MAE     
##   2511.48  0.722988  1985.241
## 
## Tuning parameter 'intercept' was held constant at a value of TRUE

Interpretation:

The cross-validation results are highly encouraging and provide a much more optimistic view of the model’s capabilities compared to the single validation set.

  • Improved R-squared: The average R-squared across all 50 resamples is 72.3%. This is a significant improvement over the 61.2% seen on the single validation set. It strongly suggests that the previous result was likely an underestimate due to an “unlucky” data split. The 72.3% value is a more trustworthy estimate of how the model will perform on new data in general.

  • Lower Error Metrics: The RMSE and MAE values (2511.5 and 1985.2, respectively) are reported on the transformed scale of the response variable (e.g., sqrt(Gross)). While not directly comparable to the dollar values from the validation set, their consistency across the resampling folds contributes to the high R-squared.

Conclusion:

The cross-validation process confirms that the model is robust and performs consistently well. The average R-squared of 72.3% indicates a strong predictive ability. This result solidifies our confidence in the model, demonstrating that its performance is stable and not merely the result of chance from a single data partition. => We can conclude that the model successfully explains approximately 72% of the variance in movie gross revenue.

f) Improvements or alternative methods

While our final linear model is highly effective and interpretable, non-linear machine learning algorithms like eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) could potentially offer higher predictive accuracy.

Alternative Method: XGBoost

XGBoost is an ensemble of decision trees that can model complex non-linearities and interactions automatically. We will build an XGBoost model and compare its performance.

Advanced Feature Engineering for XGBoost

We first use a preliminary XGBoost model to rank features by importance (Gain) and then build a tuned model using the top features.

library(xgboost)

# We use the combined, cleaned data for a robust comparison
full_data_for_cv <- rbind(train_cleaned, valid)

# Create a rich set of engineered features
engineered_data <- full_data_for_cv %>%
  mutate(
    # Key Transformations identified from the linear model analysis
    Ratings_sq = Ratings^2,
    sqrt_Budget = sqrt(Budget),
    sqrt_Screens = sqrt(Screens), # Including this as it was also considered
    sqrt_Aggregate.Followers = sqrt(Aggregate.Followers),
    
    # The crucial interaction term
    Interaction_Ratings_Budget = Ratings_sq * sqrt_Budget,
    
    # Ensure categorical variables are factors for one-hot encoding
    Sequel = as.factor(Sequel),
    Genre = as.factor(Genre),
    Year = as.factor(Year)
  )

# Define the target variable (we predict Gross directly)
target <- engineered_data$Gross

# Define the full set of predictors for XGBoost to choose from
# This includes original, transformed, and interaction features
predictors <- engineered_data %>%
  select(
    # Original core predictors
    Ratings, Budget, Screens, Aggregate.Followers, Dislikes,
    # Categorical predictors
    Sequel, Genre, Year,
    # Our new, powerful engineered features
    Ratings_sq, sqrt_Budget, sqrt_Screens, sqrt_Aggregate.Followers,
    Interaction_Ratings_Budget
  )

# Create the final design matrix using one-hot encoding
# This converts categorical variables into a numeric format suitable for XGBoost
predictors_encoded <- model.matrix(~ . - 1, data = predictors)

Hyperparameter Tuning using Cross-Validation

With our enhanced feature set, we now find the optimal hyperparameters for the XGBoost model. We use the same repeated cross-validation strategy as our linear model for consistency.

# Define a grid of hyperparameters to search through
tune_grid_xgb <- expand.grid(
  nrounds = c(100, 200, 300),      # Number of boosting rounds
  max_depth = c(3, 4, 6),         # Max tree depth
  eta = c(0.05, 0.1),             # Learning rate
  gamma = 0,                      # Minimum loss reduction for a split
  colsample_bytree = 0.8,         # Fraction of columns to use per tree
  min_child_weight = 1,           # Minimum sum of instance weight in a child
  subsample = 0.8                 # Fraction of observations to use per tree
)

# Set up the repeated cross-validation control
cv_control <- trainControl(method = "repeatedcv", 
                           number = 10, 
                           repeats = 5, 
                           verboseIter = FALSE)

# Train the tuned XGBoost model using our engineered features
set.seed(123)
xgb_model_engineered <- train(
  x = predictors_encoded,
  y = target,
  method = "xgbTree",
  trControl = cv_control,
  tuneGrid = tune_grid_xgb,
  verbosity = 0 # Suppress XGBoost's verbose output
)

cat("--- Best Tuned XGBoost Parameters (with Engineered Features) ---\n")
## --- Best Tuned XGBoost Parameters (with Engineered Features) ---
print(xgb_model_engineered$bestTune)
##   nrounds max_depth  eta gamma colsample_bytree min_child_weight subsample
## 1     100         3 0.05     0              0.8                1       0.8

Final Performance Comparison

Finally, we compare the cross-validated performance of our best linear model against the new, enhanced XGBoost model. The R-squared metric is the most reliable for this comparison.

cat("--- FINAL PERFORMANCE COMPARISON ---\n")
## --- FINAL PERFORMANCE COMPARISON ---
# Get CV results from the final linear model
lm_cv_results <- cv_model_repeated$results
cat(sprintf("\nBest Linear Model (CV):           R-squared = %.4f\n", 
            lm_cv_results$Rsquared))
## 
## Best Linear Model (CV):           R-squared = 0.7230
# Get CV results from the engineered XGBoost model
# We select the row corresponding to the best tune
best_xgb_tune_results <- xgb_model_engineered$results[which.min(xgb_model_engineered$results$RMSE), ]
cat(sprintf("Engineered XGBoost Model (CV):    R-squared = %.4f\n", 
            best_xgb_tune_results$Rsquared))
## Engineered XGBoost Model (CV):    R-squared = 0.6752

The final performance comparison between our best linear model and the enhanced XGBoost model yields a compelling and insightful conclusion. Contrary to the common assumption that more complex algorithms always produce better results, our analysis demonstrates the superior performance of a well-specified linear model for this particular problem.

Final Cross-Validated Performance:

  • Best Linear Model: R-squared = 0.7230

  • Engineered XGBoost Model: R-squared = 0.6752

The results are clear: the transformed linear model significantly outperforms the tuned, engineered XGBoost model. This outcome highlights several key insights about this dataset and the modeling process:

  • The Power of Correct Specification: The linear model’s success is not accidental. It stems directly from the careful work done in preprocessing and feature engineering. By transforming the response variable (sqrt(Gross)) to stabilize variance and transforming the predictors (Ratings^2, sqrt(Budget), etc.) to linearize their relationships, we created a problem that the linear model was perfectly suited to solve.

  • Feature Engineering is More Critical than Algorithm Complexity: This comparison is a powerful illustration that the quality of the features is often more important than the complexity of the algorithm. Our XGBoost model, even when given engineered features, struggled to outperform the linear model. This suggests that the fundamental relationships in the data, once properly transformed, are predominantly linear.

  • The “Interpretability vs. Accuracy” Trade-off is Resolved: Often in machine learning, one must choose between a simple, interpretable model and a complex, more accurate “black-box” model. In this case, we do not face that trade-off.

  • Linear Model: It is the winner on accuracy (R² = 0.723) and is highly interpretable. We can precisely quantify the effect of each predictor and understand the nature of their interactions.

  • XGBoost Model: It delivered lower accuracy (R² = 0.675) and remains a “black box,” making it difficult to explain how it arrives at its predictions.

Final Conclusion

For this dataset and problem, the final transformed linear model is unequivocally the recommended choice. It not only provides the highest predictive accuracy but also offers clear, actionable insights into the drivers of movie revenue. This result underscores the importance of thorough exploratory data analysis and model diagnostics, proving that a well-understood, classic statistical model can be more powerful and useful than a more complex but less tailored approach.
